speaker: interviewer
text: 1996, The survivor is Boris Chartan. The interview is taking place in Plainview, New York, United States of America. The interviewer is myself, Anne Bernard. And the interview is taking place in English. Let's pick up where we left off on when you were liberated, and you were together with your mother, father, and grandfather. What happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: When we were liberated in Palikrowy, in that village. With me was my mom, and dad, and my grandfather, and Junap Olaska, the cousin that we brought up the last few months to hide out with us. It was a cold winter. That March, it's still pretty cold up in that part. And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] that Thursday that we were liberated, it was just a day of walking around, like in a daze, and not realizing what happened. And Friday morning, my dad, and I, and Junap, decided to go to Podkamien, which was about five kilometers away, and see what's doing there, if there were any Jews, or you know, whatever's left, and to see. In spite of-- my mom was worried, because, you know, the uncertainty of what could happen. Yet, we went. We came into town. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] The town was also liberated already. There was a lot of Russian army in there. A few Jews were wandering around from my town. We found-- one was a family, [NON-ENGLISH], a mother and two children which have survived, much older than I am, the son. The-- [NON-ENGLISH] is probably about five or six years older than I am. And they lived right next door to us-- I mean, next house. And we stayed and rejoiced a little bit, you know, finding a few there alive. We immediately saw there's no reason to stay in Podkamien. We returned to Palikrowy, where my mother was. The reason my mom didn't go is because from all these months in the bunker, she couldn't walk on her feet. It Was very hard for her to walk. We stayed there on Saturday in Palikrowy. On Sunday, we got worried that the Germans might come back, you know, because there was moving back and forth. In fact, the Germans had fortified themselves just about 10 kilometers outside of Podkamien. So we felt the best thing to do was to start moving further away from there. And most of the few Jews that survived, and even the Polish man, Mr. Marczeniek was frightened. We all started moving back towards [INAUDIBLE], which was about, I'd say, 30 kilometers. It was like an exodus. I would recall probably about 100 people going towards Krzemieniec. We arrived there sometimes on Monday. And we were still not happy to stay there. We figured to go in a little bit further, deeper, away from the front lines, because we heard that Brody, which was almost parallel, it's really bad. The Russians cannot make any headway. So [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] we went in as far as Dubno, which was a town that my mother's sister got married lived there in Dubno. We got to Dubno about a day and a half later.

speaker: interviewer
text: What kind of transportation did you have?

speaker: interviewee
text: A horse and wagon, and mostly walking, because there wasn't enough wagons at take us. So those that were able, were just walking behind the wagon. And we got to Dubno. Again, we came into town. The town was completely no Jews. We had hoped that maybe that we'd find my mother's sister. We realized that that's not going to come to be. We knew where she lived, because they were a very wealthy family that she married into. They had a big wholesale grocery business. And the only one that we were-- came upon, is a-- her husband's [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] nephew that survived. His name was Jack. And the reason he survived is not-- he had ran away before. And he joined the Russian militia [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] as a young boy. He was in a Russian uniform. And he knew that his mother and dad were wiped out, and everybody. He was also an only child. And we stayed in Dubno approximately-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] coming in there, I'd say, April, the beginning of April, the first week of April. A lot of Jews did come, started settling in that town. My estimation would be that, probably, as they were kept coming, that we wound up in Dubno about 100 to 150 Jewish families. One of the surviving-- luckily, the rabbi from Brody, Rabbi Steinberg, Moses Steinberg, which was a very famous Hasidic family, survived. He was the [INAUDIBLE] Brody for years. He's-- he later emigrated here to America. And he was living on the west side. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] We cleaned out the shul in Dubno to make a back-- bring it back to some semblence. It was all destroyed. And he was like the rabbi in the community for these 150 families. My job was to go out once a week to collect some money so Rabbi Steinberg could live. I did that for the whole time that I stayed in Dubno. And we became very close. Matter of cat, when I got married here, he was the rabbi that married me. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] He and his wife survived. They had no children. They were a childless family.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long did you stay in Dubno?

speaker: interviewee
text: We stayed in Dubno from 1944 in spring [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] till [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] probably January or February of 1945. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] The reason that we left Dubno, we did not want to wind up in the Russians, again, because we knew that it wasn't a way of life. It wasn't a way to stay. We were able to-- they were going to take us to further deep into Poland. So our hope was to get over somehow to the German side. So we boarded a train, an open train. And we traveled from Dubno through Czechoslovakia-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] no, no, from Dubno, we traveled into [NON-ENGLISH], which was in Poland. We arrived there. And we got off the train, again, about I'd say 30, 40 Jewish families that we travelled with. And we settled in [NON-ENGLISH], which was near Katowice, was the big-- very big town. And [NON-ENGLISH] was a nice sized town. And we stayed there. Again, we found a place to live in. And little by little, the town-- again, [NON-ENGLISH] started to fill up with Jews that were running away from that part of the Ukraine, getting in deeper, closer to the Czechoslovakia border. Actually, the next border would have been Czechoslovakia, and into Germany.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you lived during this time, with food and supplies?

speaker: interviewee
text: What my dad did-- this-- [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] little by little, they started like handling, and doing a little business, and just to support themselves. It was just eking out and make a living. I mean, it was nothing substantial. You didn't really set up home. It was just a way of, again, surviving, and trying to make the best of it, knowing that, eventually, you want to get to a place, a destination, whether it would be Israel of America. That was the hope then that everybody had in mind, is to get out of Europe.

speaker: interviewer
text: So where did you finally settle there? In

speaker: interviewee
text: In [NON-ENGLISH]?

speaker: interviewer
text: After that.

speaker: interviewee
text: After [NON-ENGLISH], we, again, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] through the Joint Distribution Committee, which were emissaries that were coming over, talking to us, and explaining us different people, we were able to make a transport to go to Munich, which was Germany. And we boarded a train [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] to Munich. I remember going through Prague, Czechoslovakia, up to Pilzen. Pilzen was the famous Pilzen beer that you hear a lot about. Well, we got there, the Russians did not let us go through the border. They took us off the trains. And there was a camp they put us in. It was not a concentration camp. It was just like a holding pen. And they kept us there. We arrived there on a Friday. And there was negotiations going on between the Joint Distribution and the Russians, whether they would let us go any further. They didn't. And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] they finally took us off the train. And they put us into this camp. And in that camp, I stayed there about two weeks, while negotiations were going on, whether we are going to go further. At one point, the Americans used to send over big trucks, and supplies, to the Russians. That's vivid in my mind, those books Studebaker trucks that we used to see go through on trains. And after they dropped off that stuff in Pilzen, and one day they had negotiated they would let us go through. We boarded that train. They were just transporting trucks, and open train. So we got in. They let us board these trains. And we arrived in Munich, in the [GERMAN], which was actually-- the [GERMAN] during the Nazi time was a military camp in Munich. And when you got there, there were thousands of Jews. And it was just-- it wasn't a place that you could stay permanent. It was just gathering, gathering, Jews that were coming in from all over. And we were sent to Camp [GERMAN], which is near [GERMAN] in the camp. There was like, by train, an hour's ride from Munich. We had different satellites, where there was displaced persons camps. We arrived in [GERMAN], in that camp.

speaker: interviewer
text: Just-- who was we?

speaker: interviewee
text: My mom and dad, my cousin, my father's one brother, because one brother had died right after the war of typhus. I did not mention to you that in Dubno, as soon as we got to Dubno, my mom had typhus. I had typhus. And-- but my uncle did not make it through, because of the high temperature.

speaker: interviewer
text: How were you cured, you and your mother?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was taken to a small hospital in Dubno, which was mom stayed home. And it was just a question would-- those days, type of aspirin just to bring your temperature down. And there was nothing else for it. And I was just lucky, probably, that I survived. They immediately shave off your hair and all that, because that comes from uncleanliness, and all that stuff. So I remember having my head shaved, and that sort of stuff.

speaker: interviewer
text: So when you arrived at Foehrenwald?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, there was a big displaced person's camp. You got housing. Every family, you got just about one room. You did not get two rooms or three rooms, which I can't blame them. It was-- there was very little available. And we just lived in that one room. And the bathroom facilities were out in the group. But there was already water facilities, and all that sort of stuff.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where was your grandfather?

speaker: interviewee
text: My grandfather was in Foehrenwald with us too.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what did you do? Did you have a job there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, if you wanted, you could go to work. There was no forced work. I chose to go to work in the warehouse that was distributing the food to the people in the camp. And my dad worked in there too. We both went to work daily. And it was just unloading trucks of food that were brought in, opening up the cartons, and shelving it out. And then people would come. And the kitchen facilities was available. But if you didn't want to, you just got your rationing. You could take it back to your living quarters, and get your portions. And there was a kosher kitchen and an un-kosher kitchen in Foehrenwald. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I remember the first time, which I had never seen because in our part of the world, there was very little cannery stuff. Everything was fresh, living in these small towns. I didn't realize what a can of spam meant. I read it. [LAUGHS] It looked good then. But-- and it was-- a lot of the rationing stuff that they got was because of what was left over from the army stuff that they did not use. So it was just loads and loads of stuff. And they kept bringing it in every day through the operations of UNRRA. And [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] the joint distribution was very heavily involved in it. And one of them that was outstanding in my mind was-- that I have seen, the mayor of New York, Fiorello La Guardia, that came to camp to see, you know, how we're living, which he was concerned. I think at that time, he maybe even represented the UNRRA. That was his mission. That's why he was there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you realize who he was when he was there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not really, no. Later on, you know, when I read Fiorello La Guardia was there. To us, he was a great American. And, you know.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you happy in that camp?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, in that camp already there was a little life to camp, because we started to do our own cultural things. And [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] entertain ourselves. I joined a group, a theater group. And we used to go out, and put on little skits. And after that-- I guess we got so good, so we went to go to other camps. So I remember going out of Foehrenwald and going to different camps. Near Nuremberg and Frankfurt, I traveled. I did a little traveling while I was in Germany, going to visit other DP camps. Because you didn't have to-- if you don't want to go to work one day, there was nothing they're going to dock you your pay. You didn't get paid. It was all volunteerism. It was good.

speaker: interviewer
text: So how did you manage to pay for the transportation to go, for example, to different cities in Germany?

speaker: interviewee
text: In those days, we did not pay the-- the-- it was-- when you look back today, I don't know if we did the right thing or we did the wrong thing. We just literally refused to pay. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] We were angry. I'll never forget. I traveled with my dad and my uncle from Munich to Frankfurt, which is a night's-- one night trip overnight. You boarded the train at 7:00. You arrived 7:00 in the morning. It was a 12-hour trip. And you just got on the train. The conductor started screaming. And the whole train was full of Jews, because the Germans were a little afraid to get among us. They didn't know what to expect. And I remember a couple young-- my friends, just taking a to conductor, and threw him right off the train. And the train kept riding. And we just-- we didn't pay. We refused to pay. We used to tell them, [GERMAN], and just go to hell.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long did you stay in Foehrenwald?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Foehrenwald, I stayed-- came down '45. I stayed till 1946, July. We had made contact with-- my dad had a sister in Montreal living. And my grandfather Chartan had a couple of brothers here, which was my dad's uncles. They worked out the papers for us to come. And in July of 1946, everything was worked out to go to America. And we had to go to Bremerhaven. And you had to wait like three weeks before you got on a transport, because I believe that-- I know the ship I came on, the Marie Fletcher. And I think it was the second trip of Jews that were brought to America. I was on the-- we were on the second trip. We arrived here on a Monday, in July 15th, 1946.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you pass the Statue of Liberty when you came into the harbor?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Did I know what it was? No. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] It was just-- the boat was full of refugees, all refugees, a matter of fact.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you have any recollection of your feelings when you came to New York?

speaker: interviewee
text: Anxiety. I knew some of my cousins from pictures, from Montreal, really. And I knew that my aunt and my uncle, which name is Propst, P-R-O-P-S-T, would be awaiting us her, and their son, my first cousin Morris, they were here. And they came down to the boat. And they took us off the boat. And they took us to my-- to the Bronx on 180th Street, or 181st Street, to my uncle's home, because my mother also had uncles here from her mother's side. Name was [NON-ENGLISH]. And they heard about us. So they came that evening to meet us all.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was that like for you back then?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was a little frightening, coming into such a big city. I almost thought that I wanted to go back to Foehrenwald, to that little comfortable feeling, where you're among your own friends. And It's difficult. Everybody was speaking English. And-- [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] well, everybody was very nice to you. It was a sort of strange feeling.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you speak any English?

speaker: interviewee
text: A little, a little, not enough. My uncles proceeded to set us up in an apartment. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Within probably two weeks, we were set up on the east side, at 95 Colombia Street, which is between Rivington and-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I can't remember. It's 95 Colombia Street, right off Rivington.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you do when you arrived in your own home?

speaker: interviewee
text: They bought us furniture. They took us out. They bought us furniture. They set up the apartment. And I proceeded to go get a job. And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] my uncle, Sam Chartan, which was in the Bronx, had made a connection to a friend of his, which a son had a restaurant on 39th Street in New York. And it was called Harry and Walter's, that I remember. They took me there one morning. And they introduced me to Streamline Button Company. That was the Walters, the people-- he was two doors away from the company. And he had asked if they would hire me. And Mr. [INAUDIBLE], which was the owner of the Streamline Button Company, gave me a job. And I went to work there, almost probably-- mostly if I recall, three weeks after I was here, maybe even sooner.

speaker: interviewer
text: How much did you make a week?

speaker: interviewer
text: And how many hours did you put in?

speaker: interviewee
text: Put in about 48 hours. I was happy. I was happy, because I knew that I can support-- my father did not get a job so quick. And I remember just coming home, and taking out the $12, and just putting it on my mother's table, and giving it to her.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did that seem like a lot of money to you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, yes, I did-- I wouldn't spend it. I sometimes even decided to walk, which is some work if you know, from Valencia Street, all the way up to 39th Street. I was Young. So rather than spend a dime for the subway, I used to take that walk. Maybe at night, I took a ride home. And my mother would, most of the, time give me a sandwich. Of course, I could go into Harry and Walter's if I wanted to eat. That would have been no problem. But I didn't. I didn't feel comfortable. Most of the time, I'd take a sandwich to work. It would be the same sandwich, a scrambled egg on a roll, with a bottle of Pepsi that I'd buy myself. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: What kind of work did you do at Streamline?

speaker: interviewee
text: When I came in there, first they-- not knowing the language, I used to do mostly packaging and shipping. I was put in the shipping department, where they-- a fella that-- his name was Abe. And he lived on the east side. I didn't realize that he, too, lived on the east side. And after I-- he was like my boss in the shipping department, became very friendly. And I worked very hard. He took a liking to me.

speaker: interviewee
text: So he started offering-- he used to give me a ride to work. And the outstanding thing, that he had a Hudson. That was the first that-- [LAUGHS] that stood out in my mind, was his Hudson. I think it was a 1946 Hudson that he drove, used to take me to work.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I liked it. And I worked very hard it. I think it was recognized for my hard work. And Mr. [INAUDIBLE] was a very fine mane. And he sort of guided me, and gave me some opportunities while I was there. But after a year, he suggested that I go out selling, rather than just be a shipping clerk. And that was frightening. But he convinced me that-- I don't think he did it mainly to make a-- to be his salesman, but probably gave me the opportunity to introduce me to the outside world, to go out and mingle with people. And when I look back now, I realize that's what he probably had in mind. He was a very good person. He was very charitable. he was very involved in UJA in those days. And I got to know his family. And I was his trusted worker that if he had to go pick up a package that was clothes or something, Marti Walker's, which was the expensive store, I was the trusted guy to go into Marti Walker's.

speaker: interviewee
text: My daytime, I used to take a walk up to 7th Avenue on 39th Street. And making $12 a week, and looking in those terrific stores, I used to, like, go up to the window. And I'd just stare in the window, and say to myself, how am I going to buy a tie for $15 when you make $12 a week? Will you ever have this? And it became, like, almost drawing you to the window. And this one day, I got a tap on the shoulder. And there was Mr. [INAUDIBLE]. He was coming back, probably, from a meeting, or lunch. And he says, how you doing? I says, OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: He says, I want to see you in the afternoon when you come back from lunch. And I figured, oh, I got probably a pink slip going not for working. I came into the office. And he said to me, you may not know, he says, but I've seen you in front of that windows a lot of times.
